articles.title: Siblings Compete in Race Across the World
articles.summary: Katie and Harrison, siblings from Manchester, participated in the BBC show Race Across the World, where teams travel over 12,000 km across Europe and Asia without phones and with a limited daily budget of less than £26 each, supplemented by odd jobs. Katie was initially nervous and in denial about joining, but Harrison persuaded her. The show involves physical and emotional challenges, such as missed connections and navigation errors, but it offers opportunities for bonding and exploration. Katie found that spending time with her brother was easy due to their deep understanding of each other. She believes their bickering and mean behavior actually strengthen their relationship, contrasting with stereotypes of sibling conflict. The article highlights how the show tests resilience and teamwork while fostering personal connections.

articles.title: Hidden Costs: The Subscription Trap Dilemma
articles.summary: Neha's experience with LiveCareer, an online CV builder, highlights the issue of subscription traps. She initially paid for a one-time service to download her CV but later discovered she had been enrolled in a monthly subscription, resulting in over £500 being deducted from her joint account over two years. Her husband assumed she had authorized the payments, so they went unnoticed. Neha attempted to cancel and seek a refund, but faced challenges because LiveCareer is an American company, limiting her recourse to UK consumer protections like Small Claims Court or Trading Standards. She is exploring a chargeback through her bank. This story is part of a broader trend where many consumers are caught by direct debits for subscriptions they forgot or didn't realize they had signed up for, such as for security cameras or meal kits. The UK government has announced a clampdown on such practices, prompting people to share their stories with BBC Your Voice. LiveCareer claims transparency in its subscription terms, but Neha argues that companies often make it easy to continue taking money without clear communication. The article underscores the importance of vigilance in checking bank statements and the need for stronger consumer safeguards.

articles.title: Legal Battle: Judge Dismisses Majority of Blake Lively's Harassment Claims Against Justin Baldoni
articles.summary: In a significant legal development, a federal judge has dismissed the majority of claims in a sexual harassment lawsuit filed by actress Blake Lively against her co-star Justin Baldoni. The lawsuit, initiated in 2024, accused Baldoni of harassment and orchestrating a smear campaign during the production of the film It Ends with Us. Judge Lewis Liman ruled to throw out 10 out of 13 allegations, including those for harassment and defamation, citing various legal reasons in a detailed 152-page opinion. However, three claims—breach of contract, retaliation, and aiding and abetting in retaliation—were upheld and are set to proceed to a civil trial in New York on May 18. Lively's legal representative, Sigrid McCawley, emphasized that the case centers on retaliation and reputational damage, noting that the harassment claims were dropped due to technicalities rather than a lack of wrongdoing. Baldoni had previously filed a $400 million counter-lawsuit against Lively, which was dismissed last year. The dispute originates from on-set interactions, with Lively alleging that Baldoni and his studio, Wayfarer, engaged in social media manipulation and media tactics to harm her reputation. Lively plans to testify at the trial to highlight these issues.

articles.title: Harry Clark's Journey to Meet the Pope for Faith and Mental Health
articles.summary: Harry Clark, winner of The Traitors, embarked on a pilgrimage to Rome as part of his BBC documentary, Harry Clark Goes to Rome, aiming to address his questions about Catholicism. Raised Catholic, Clark faced significant struggles with his faith and mental health after joining the Army at 16, where he witnessed trauma and lost friends to mental health issues. His personal life further deteriorated with the end of a long-term relationship and a brain injury from an attack, leading him to contemplate suicide. Upon his mother's advice, he resumed praying, which fostered gratitude and a renewed connection to his faith. Determined to meet Pope Leo XIV, elected in 2025, Clark sought to understand what defines a good or bad Catholic. Despite challenges like being hung up on by the Vatican and sending direct messages, he succeeded in arranging a meeting. During their encounter, they discussed mental health and memes, providing Clark with a transformative experience that reinforced his spiritual journey and highlighted the importance of seeking support during crises.

articles.title: New Peaky Blinders Series Features Jamie Bell in 1950s Birmingham
articles.summary: A new television series of Peaky Blinders has been announced, starring Jamie Bell as Duke Shelby, the oldest son of Tommy Shelby. Set in the 1950s, ten years after the recent movie Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man, the show will delve into the impact of World War Two on Birmingham, portraying Duke as an older, wiser, and more dangerous character. The cast includes notable actors such as Charlie Heaton from Stranger Things, Jessica Brown Findlay from Downton Abbey, and Lashana Lynch from The Day Of The Jackal, with Lucy Karczewski making her TV debut. Creator Steven Knight expressed enthusiasm for this new era, highlighting the move to post-war Birmingham. Production is underway at Digbeth Loc. Studios in Birmingham, supported by a BBC partnership with the West Midlands Combined Authority and Create Central, backed by a £40 million annual investment. The series consists of two parts, each with six hour-long episodes, and will premiere on BBC iPlayer, BBC One in the UK, and Netflix globally, marking a significant expansion in BBC production efforts.

articles.title: Revolutionizing Wireless with Laser-Powered Light Communication
articles.summary: Researchers have developed an optical wireless communication system that achieves 362.7 gigabits per second using a compact chip with a 5x5 array of vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers (VCSELs). This technology uses light instead of radio waves, offering higher bandwidth and avoiding interference in crowded indoor environments like offices and data centers. The system is energy-efficient, using half the power of traditional Wi-Fi, and features an optical design that shapes light beams into a grid to prevent overlap and support multiple users. Published in Advanced Photonics Nexus, the study demonstrates scalable potential for high-speed indoor wireless, with testing showing stable performance over two meters. Limitations include receiver bandwidth, but advancements could push speeds even higher, making it promising for applications like virtual reality and streaming.
